name: New Hampshire
examName: New Hampshire Real Estate Salesperson Examination

examFacts.notes: Administered by PSI for the New Hampshire Real Estate Commission, which sits within the Office of Professional Licensure and Certification (OPLC).

intro:
The New Hampshire Real Estate Commission (NHREC) grades the 40-question state portion on its own, so a strong national score won't rescue a weak state score. That makes New Hampshire-specific preparation matter as much as the national material.

New Hampshire repealed its Real Estate Recovery Fund effective September 1, 2023, and shifted consumer protection to a $25,000 principal-broker surety bond under RSA 331-A:14. The state recognizes five distinct brokerage relationships under RSA 331-A:25-b through :25-f, including the facilitator role that very few states use. RSA 477:4-c forces sellers to disclose private water-supply and sewage information before contract, the RSA 78-B transfer tax splits $0.75 per $100 between buyer and seller, and RSA 354-A's Law Against Discrimination piles state-specific protected classes on top of federal fair housing. None of that looks like the material a candidate in a neighboring state studies. A generic real estate practice test won't cut it. You need New Hampshire-specific practice questions.

faq.q: How many questions are on the New Hampshire real estate exam?
faq.a: The New Hampshire Real Estate Salesperson Examination has 120 scored multiple-choice questions. PSI splits the test into an 80-question national portion and a 40-question New Hampshire portion, and they are scored separately.

faq.q: What's the passing score for the New Hampshire real estate exam?
faq.a: 70% on each portion, scored independently. That works out to at least 56 of 80 correct on the national portion and at least 28 of 40 correct on the New Hampshire portion. You have to clear both bars in the same sitting, so a strong national score will not rescue a weak state score.

faq.q: How much does the New Hampshire real estate exam cost?
faq.a: $67 for a first attempt and $65 for any retake, paid to PSI when you schedule. That's separate from the New Hampshire Office of Professional Licensure and Certification license fee you'll pay after you pass.

faq.q: How long is the New Hampshire real estate exam?
faq.a: Four hours total. PSI gives you 150 minutes for the 80 national questions and 90 minutes for the 40 New Hampshire questions, and you find out whether you passed each portion before you leave the test center.

faq.q: Who administers the New Hampshire real estate exam?
faq.a: The New Hampshire Real Estate Salesperson Examination is administered by PSI for the New Hampshire Real Estate Commission. You register and schedule your exam date through PSI.

faq.q: Is the New Hampshire real estate exam hard?
faq.a: Most candidates underestimate the state portion. The national side is the same shared content New Hampshire candidates see in every state, and a decent prep course will get you there. The 40 state questions are where people lose. You're tested on the 2023 Recovery Fund repeal and the $25,000 principal-broker surety bond, the five brokerage relationships in RSA 331-A:25-b through :25-f including New Hampshire's facilitator role, RSA 477:4-c water and sewage disclosure, the RSA 78-B transfer tax, RSA 354-A's Law Against Discrimination, RSA 205-A manufactured-housing-park rights, and the NHREC's Rea 400 and Rea 700 administrative rules on advertising, trust accounts, and disclosure timing. Honest practice on New Hampshire-specific material is the difference between passing on your first $67 attempt and retaking.

faq.q: What's on the New Hampshire real estate exam?
faq.a: The 80-question national portion covers property ownership, land use, financing, agency and fiduciary duties, valuation, contracts, transfer of title, practice of real estate, and basic real estate math. The 40-question New Hampshire portion tests the Real Estate Commission and OPLC, RSA 331-A licensee conduct and the post-2023 surety-bond framework, the NHREC's Rea administrative rules (advertising under Rea 404.05, trust accounts and disclosure timing under Rea 701 and 702), the five RSA 331-A:25 brokerage relationships including disclosed dual agency and the facilitator role, RSA 477:4-c property condition disclosures, RSA 78-B transfer tax, RSA 354-A fair housing, RSA 540 landlord-tenant practice, RSA 356-B condominium registration, RSA 205-A manufactured-housing-park tenant rights, and New Hampshire planning and zoning under RSA 672 and RSA 674.

faq.q: What's the best way to prepare for the New Hampshire real estate exam?
faq.a: After you finish the 40-hour OPLC-approved pre-license course, the highest-leverage thing you can do is grind through New Hampshire-specific practice questions with honest feedback on every miss. National practice tests will help you on 80 questions but will not catch you on RSA 331-A, RSA 477:4-c, the Rea rules, the post-Recovery-Fund framework, or RSA 205-A. The RealReady app gives you the full New Hampshire question bank plus the shared national bank, a missed-question drill mode, and per-category progress tracking so you can see exactly where the state portion is leaking points. Most users study 30 to 60 minutes a day for 2 to 4 weeks before sitting the PSI exam.

The 20 New Hampshire practice questions on this page are free, no account or signup needed. Unlocking the rest of the New Hampshire bank is a single in-app purchase you pay once and own, with no recurring charge and no time limit on access.

faq.q: Does RealReady cover the New Hampshire real estate exam?
faq.a: Yes. RealReady has a dedicated New Hampshire question bank built for the New Hampshire real estate exam, covering the national principles every candidate sees plus the New Hampshire law that trips people up, like the $25,000 principal-broker surety bond under RSA 331-A:14, the five brokerage relationships under RSA 331-A including the facilitator role, and RSA 354-A's Law Against Discrimination.

faq.q: Is RealReady a real estate license course?
faq.a: No. RealReady is exam practice, not a pre-license course, so it doesn't count toward New Hampshire's required pre-license education hours. Use it after or alongside your coursework to drill realistic practice questions and find your weak spots before exam day.
